knownFor: Canadian actor, jazz musician and voice artist (Sabretooth in X-Men, Mok Swagger in Rock & Rule).
reveal: Boba Fett spoke his very first words not in a film but in the animated segment of 1978's much-maligned Star Wars Holiday Special, and the debut voice of the galaxy's most iconic bounty hunter was Canadian actor Don Francks.

reveal: The icy Imperial Governor Arihnda Pryce who menaced the Ghost crew through two seasons of Star Wars Rebels was voiced by Mary Elizabeth McGlynn, better known as the anime-dub voice of Major Kusanagi.

reveal: The young High Republic Jedi Bell Zettifar in the animated preschool series Young Jedi Adventures is voiced by Marcus Scribner, Junior from black-ish.

knownFor: Teen actress from Avengers: Infinity War (young Gamora) and Barbie.

knownFor: American painter and character actress who physically portrayed the Emperor's hologram in the original theatrical cut of The Empire Strikes Back.
reveal: The Emperor who first menaced Vader was a 78-year-old woman: painter-actress Marjorie Eaton, buried under makeup with chimpanzee eyes optically superimposed and a man's voice dubbed over her. She went uncredited and was quietly replaced by Ian McDiarmid in the 2004 re-release.

knownFor: American character actor who was the original, long-uncredited voice of bounty hunter Boba Fett in The Empire Strikes Back.
reveal: The menacing drawl of the original Boba Fett belonged to Jason Wingreen, better known as Harry the bartender on All in the Family. He went uncredited for two decades before his lines were re-dubbed by Temuera Morrison in 2004.

knownFor: New Zealand stage and screen actor who supplied the voice of the Emperor's hologram in the original theatrical cut of The Empire Strikes Back.
reveal: Before Ian McDiarmid ever donned the robes, the Emperor's rasping hologram was voiced by veteran actor Clive Revill, dubbed over a silent stand-in and scrubbed from the film in the 2004 re-release.

knownFor: British-American actor who voiced Chancellor Palpatine and Darth Sidious across the animated Star Wars: The Clone Wars until his death.
reveal: The silky menace of the animated Sidious was Ian Abercrombie, the same actor audiences knew as Seinfeld's fussy Mr. Pitt, voicing the Sith Lord right up until he died in 2012.

knownFor: American actor who voiced the young rebel Ezra Bridger across all four seasons of the animated Star Wars Rebels.
reveal: The Force-sensitive street kid Ezra Bridger was given voice by Taylor Gray, a live-action TV actor who never once appears on screen in the series he anchors.

knownFor: American actor who played Rebel gunner Dak Ralter and doubled inside the Boba Fett armor for the Cloud City scenes of The Empire Strikes Back.
reveal: When Jeremy Bulloch was pulled away to play an Imperial officer, the Boba Fett confronting Vader in the Bespin corridor was actually John Morton, the same man seen unmasked moments earlier as Luke's doomed snowspeeder gunner, Dak.

knownFor: Brazilian-American capoeira martial artist and Emmy-winning stunt performer who fights inside the beskar armor whenever Din Djarin throws a punch.
reveal: Every acrobatic, armor-clad brawl in The Mandalorian is Lateef Crowder dos Santos under the helmet, not Pedro Pascal, a capoeira stuntman who won a Primetime Emmy for a role in which his face is never once seen.

knownFor: Prolific American voice actor, familiar to gamers as Victor "Sully" Sullivan across the Uncharted series.
reveal: The wheezing four-armed cyborg general of the 2D Clone Wars micro-series is voiced by Richard McGonagle — the same warm gravel behind Nathan Drake's cigar-chomping mentor Sully.

knownFor: The original voice of accident-prone pilot Launchpad McQuack on DuckTales and Darkwing Duck.
reveal: The Mon Calamari admiral barking orders in Rogue Squadron is Terry McGovern — voice of Launchpad McQuack, and the man whose ad-libbed "I think I ran over a wookiee back there" on THX 1138 handed George Lucas the word.

production: Star Wars: Rogue Squadron II: Rogue Leader
knownFor: Veteran LucasArts video game composer known for Grim Fandango and the Sly Cooper series.

knownFor: American voice actor also heard as Rafael Diaz in Star vs. the Forces of Evil and Shang Tsung in the Mortal Kombat Legends films.

knownFor: Canadian voice actress famous as Princess Celestia in My Little Pony: Friendship Is Magic.

reveal: As Dukat, Marc Alaimo sat for hours of Cardassian scale and neck-ridge prosthetic application before shooting days across DS9's run. The face audiences came to fear as the Cardassian prefect of Bajor was never his own.

knownFor: Marc Alaimo is best known for playing Gul Dukat, one of Deep Space Nine's central antagonists, across the series' seven-season run.
reveal: Marc Alaimo returned to Cardassian prosthetics to play Gul Macet, Dukat's cousin, letting the same actor appear opposite his own signature character. The double-casting only worked because the makeup, not Alaimo's own face, was doing the identifying.

knownFor: Marc Alaimo is best known for playing Gul Dukat, one of Deep Space Nine's central antagonists, across the series' seven-season run.
reveal: Years before Cardassian ridges became his signature, Marc Alaimo played the Romulan commander Tebok under a simpler set of pointed ears and a heavy brow appliance. It's one of his earliest Star Trek appearances, well before Dukat made him a franchise fixture.

knownFor: Rick Worthy is a prolific character actor widely known for playing the Cylon Simon on Battlestar Galactica and Blaine DeBeers on iZombie, as well as the Klingon leader T'Kuvma in Star Trek: Discovery.
reveal: Under TNG-era Klingon forehead prosthetics, Rick Worthy played Kornan, the Klingon who presides over Alexander's Rite of Ascension in the episode "Firstborn." Worthy has spent much of his career acting under designed faces across multiple science-fiction franchises, later returning to Star Trek in heavier prosthetics as Discovery's T'Kuvma.

knownFor: Rick Worthy is a prolific character actor widely known for playing the Cylon Simon on Battlestar Galactica and Blaine DeBeers on iZombie, as well as the Klingon leader T'Kuvma in Star Trek: Discovery.
reveal: Beneath Xindi-Primate facial prosthetics, Rick Worthy played Jannar, a member of the Xindi Council who works alongside Degra during the season-long Xindi arc of Star Trek: Enterprise. It's one of several designed-face roles across his career, which also spans a Cylon on Battlestar Galactica and a Klingon on Star Trek: Discovery.

knownFor: Best known for playing President Charles Logan on the Fox series 24.

The standard Cardassian appliance work of the DS9 makeup department covers his face, leaving only his voice and line readings as himself.

knownFor: Best known for playing President Charles Logan on the Fox series 24.
reveal: Under the pointed ears and upswept brow of Sopek, Gregory Itzin plays a rigid, by-the-book Vulcan investigator alongside Ezri Dax. The standard Vulcan appliance work covers his ears and brow line while his own eyes and delivery carry the performance.

knownFor: A prolific genre character actor best known as the Alien Bounty Hunter on The X-Files and as the leather-clad biker whose body and clothes the T-1000 copies in Terminator 2: Judgment Day.
reveal: Under the bald, heavily ridged Dosi head prosthetic, Brian Thompson appears as one of the alien traders bargaining with the Ferengi and the Grand Nagus. The full facial and cranial appliance work is typical of his long run of Star Trek guest spots playing one designed alien after another.

knownFor: A prolific genre character actor best known as the Alien Bounty Hunter on The X-Files and as the leather-clad biker whose body and clothes the T-1000 copies in Terminator 2: Judgment Day.
reveal: Under the grey, veined Jem'Hadar head prosthetic, Brian Thompson plays one of the Dominion's soldiers introduced as the Jem'Hadar threat escalates on DS9. He acts entirely through his eyes, since the full Jem'Hadar appliance covers the rest of his face and scalp.

knownFor: A prolific genre character actor best known as the Alien Bounty Hunter on The X-Files and as the leather-clad biker whose body and clothes the T-1000 copies in Terminator 2: Judgment Day.
reveal: Under the Klingon forehead ridge, Brian Thompson plays a young Klingon officer serving an exchange posting aboard the Enterprise-D. It was one of his earliest genre roles, years before he became a familiar face - almost always under makeup - across television science fiction.

knownFor: A prolific genre character actor best known as the Alien Bounty Hunter on The X-Files and as the leather-clad biker whose body and clothes the T-1000 copies in Terminator 2: Judgment Day.
reveal: Under the Romulan brow and ears, Brian Thompson plays a senior Romulan military commander during Enterprise's Romulan War storyline. The role continued his long pattern of returning to Star Trek in different alien makeup rather than as a recurring human character.

knownFor: An English actress known for starring as Abby Grant in the 1975 BBC series Survivors.
reveal: Under the Romulan brow ridge and forward-swept hairline, Carolyn Seymour plays the commander who nearly triggers an interstellar incident during a Yridian-spread computer plague. It is the first of two different Romulan commanders she played on The Next Generation, years apart, each unrecognizable as the other.

knownFor: An English actress known for starring as Abby Grant in the 1975 BBC series Survivors.
reveal: Under the same style of Romulan brow and ear appliances, Carolyn Seymour returns four years after playing Taris to portray Toreth, commander of a warbird that captures a disguised Picard. The prosthetic work covers her face completely enough that the two roles register as different people on screen.

knownFor: Suzie Plakson is a prolific genre television actress recognized for playing three different alien and human characters across Star Trek: The Next Generation.
reveal: As the Andorian commander Tarah, Plakson acts beneath blue skin makeup and prosthetic antennae. She was already a Star Trek veteran by this point, having earlier played a Klingon, a Vulcan, and a version of the omnipotent being Q on The Next Generation.

knownFor: Suzie Plakson is a prolific genre television actress recognized for playing three different alien and human characters across Star Trek: The Next Generation.
reveal: As the half-Klingon diplomat K'Ehleyr, mother of Worf's son Alexander, Plakson performs under Klingon forehead-ridge prosthetics. She would return to the franchise playing two more entirely different characters, the Vulcan doctor Selar and a female incarnation of Q.

knownFor: Alexander Siddig played Dr. Julian Bashir across all seven seasons of Star Trek: Deep Space Nine and later appeared as Doran Martell in Game of Thrones.
reveal: In the Deep Space Nine pilot, the aged, sickly-complexioned hijacker Rao Vantika is played by Alexander Siddig — the same actor introduced elsewhere in that same premiere as the station's youthful, unaltered Dr. Bashir. One performance sits under heavy prosthetic aging and illness makeup, the other bare-faced, in a single episode.

knownFor: Alexander Siddig played Dr. Julian Bashir across all seven seasons of Star Trek: Deep Space Nine and later appeared as Doran Martell in Game of Thrones.
reveal: As Torias Dax, one of Jadzia's previous hosts seen in a memory-flashback in 'Equilibrium,' Alexander Siddig trades his usual Starfleet uniform for the freckled temple spots of a joined Trill. It's a brief, single-appliance role for an actor otherwise anchored on the series as DS9's chief medical officer.

knownFor: Leland Orser is a prolific character actor known for roles in Se7en, Alien: Resurrection, Taken, and the television series ER and Fargo.
reveal: For nearly the full runtime of 'The Die Is Cast,' Leland Orser plays Lovok as a ranking Tal Shiar officer under standard Romulan forehead and ear prosthetics, indistinguishable from any other Romulan on the show. Only in the episode's closing scenes is Lovok exposed as a Founder, revealing the Romulan disguise the character — and Orser — had worn throughout.

knownFor: Leland Orser is a prolific character actor known for roles in Se7en, Alien: Resurrection, Taken, and the television series ER and Fargo.
reveal: In 'Sanctuary,' Leland Orser plays one of the Skrreean refugees under ridged, weathered full-face prosthetic makeup built to sell the species' harsh, migratory history. It's a fully disguised alien turn from an actor better known today for his extensive film and television character work.

knownFor: Duncan Regehr is best known for the title role in the syndicated series The New Adventures of Zorro (1990–1993).
reveal: Regehr's own face stays largely visible under Shakaar's single Bajoran nose-ridge appliance, a subtle piece rather than a full redesign. Off Bajor he built his career as television's Zorro and played Count Dracula in the 1987 film The Monster Squad.

knownFor: Brent Spiner is best known for playing the android Lieutenant Commander Data on Star Trek: The Next Generation and its films.
reveal: Brent Spiner, already fifteen years into playing the android Data, took on B-4 as Data's crude, damaged prototype in Star Trek: Nemesis, distinguished from Data mainly by makeup and manner rather than a different face. Spiner is a stage- and cabaret-trained actor from Houston, Texas, who joined Star Trek: The Next Generation as part of its original 1987 cast.

knownFor: Brent Spiner is best known for playing the android Lieutenant Commander Data on Star Trek: The Next Generation and its films.
reveal: Brent Spiner played both Data and his renegade twin Lore across The Next Generation, telling the androids apart largely through vocal tone and posture rather than a rebuilt face. Spiner, a veteran of Broadway and off-Broadway theater before television, brought a sly, emotive edge to Lore that contrasted with Data's flat affect.

knownFor: Brent Spiner is best known for playing the android Lieutenant Commander Data on Star Trek: The Next Generation and its films.
reveal: In the Next Generation episode 'Masks,' Brent Spiner's Data was overwritten by an ancient alien archive and cycled through a series of masked personas, including Ihat, realized with prosthetic masks built over his own face. Spiner, primarily a stage-trained actor, relied on voice and body language to keep each persona distinct once the masks covered most of his features.

knownFor: Brent Spiner is best known for playing the android Lieutenant Commander Data on Star Trek: The Next Generation and its films.
reveal: In the same 'Masks' episode, Brent Spiner's most elaborate persona was Masaka, an ancient sun-and-moon deity rendered with a heavy ceremonial mask and headdress built over Spiner's own face. Spiner, better known without any prosthetics as Lieutenant Commander Data, carried much of Masaka's performance through voice alone once the mask went on.

knownFor: Nana Visitor is best known for originating and starring as Bajoran first officer Kira Nerys across all seven seasons of Star Trek: Deep Space Nine.
reveal: Under the Bajoran nose-ridge appliance is Nana Visitor, a former dancer and stage performer who moved into television before landing the role of Kira. She was married to her Deep Space Nine co-star Alexander Siddig, who played Dr. Julian Bashir, for several years during the show's run.

knownFor: Nana Visitor is best known for playing Bajoran first officer Kira Nerys throughout Star Trek: Deep Space Nine.
reveal: The same Bajoran nose-ridge appliance and the same actress, Nana Visitor, carried over into the show's Mirror Universe episodes, where Kira's alternate-universe counterpart appeared opposite mirror versions of the regular cast. Visitor played both the prime and mirror versions of the character across the series.

knownFor: Armin Shimerman is best known for playing the Ferengi bartender Quark throughout Star Trek: Deep Space Nine, and for his recurring role as Principal Snyder on Buffy the Vampire Slayer.
reveal: Under the Ferengi prosthetics — the oversized ears, ridged cranium, and filed teeth — is Armin Shimerman, a stage-trained character actor who sat for hours of makeup application for every appearance across the show's seven seasons. Off camera he also co-wrote several Star Trek novels built around Quark and the Ferengi.

knownFor: Michael Dorn is best known for playing Lieutenant Worf across Star Trek: The Next Generation and Deep Space Nine, making him the actor with the most on-screen appearances in Star Trek history.
reveal: Under the heavy Klingon forehead prosthetics that required hours in the makeup chair is Michael Dorn, whose deep, distinctive voice carried through the ridges for more combined screen time as one character than any other actor in the franchise. Off camera he is an experienced pilot known for flying and collecting vintage aircraft.

knownFor: Ted Cassidy is best known for playing the towering butler Lurch on The Addams Family (1964-1966).
reveal: Under Ruk's grey, waxen android makeup was the 6-foot-9 Ted Cassidy, whose height and rumbling bass voice did as much to sell the artificial strongman as Fred Phillips's makeup did. Television audiences of the day already knew that voice and frame from his other, far more genial role as Lurch.

knownFor: Terry Farrell is also known for starring as Reggie Kostas on the sitcom Becker.
reveal: The elaborate Trill spot pattern running from Jadzia's hairline down her neck disguised Terry Farrell for six seasons of Deep Space Nine, reapplied for nearly every appearance she made. Off camera she had built her career as a fashion model before this became her breakout series-regular role.

knownFor: Jonathan Frakes is best known for playing William Riker across Star Trek: The Next Generation and its films, and later became a prolific Star Trek director.
reveal: For TNG's 'First Contact,' Frakes went under a reshaped prosthetic forehead and webbed-finger gloves to pass as the Malcorian tourist Rivas Jakara while Riker worked undercover on a pre-warp world. He later described the makeup as leaving him with 'plugs stuck up my nose,' made worse once the story called for his character to be beaten up on camera.

knownFor: Randy Oglesby is best known for his recurring role as Degra on Star Trek: Enterprise.
reveal: Kir's Brenari markings amount to little more than two small ridges between the eyebrows, but the makeup was enough to disguise Randy Oglesby as one of the telepathic refugees Voyager smuggled through hostile space in 'Counterpoint.' It was one of several alien guest roles that made Oglesby a recurring face in Star Trek's makeup chairs across three different series.

knownFor: Randy Oglesby is best known for his recurring role as Degra on Star Trek: Enterprise.
reveal: Silaran Prin's scarred, ridged Cardassian face and hands took a full prosthetic application to hide Randy Oglesby underneath the disfigured killer at the center of DS9's 'The Darkness and the Light.' By that point Oglesby was already a familiar face to the franchise's makeup department, having played the Miradorn twins in the show's first season.

knownFor: Randy Oglesby is best known for his recurring role as Degra on Star Trek: Enterprise.
reveal: Ah-Kel was one half of the linked Miradorn twins in DS9's first-season episode 'Vortex,' with Randy Oglesby playing both alien brothers under matching ridged prosthetic makeup. It was one of his earliest Star Trek credits, at the start of a guest-acting run that kept bringing him back to the franchise's makeup chairs for the next decade.

knownFor: Randy Oglesby is best known for his recurring role as Degra on Star Trek: Enterprise.
reveal: Ro-Kel, the twin killed early in DS9's 'Vortex,' was also played by Randy Oglesby, doubling opposite himself as Ah-Kel under matching Miradorn prosthetics. It was one of his earliest Star Trek roles, well before he became a recurring guest face across Deep Space Nine, Voyager, and Enterprise.

knownFor: Randy Oglesby is best known for his recurring role as Degra on Star Trek: Enterprise.
reveal: Trena'L, the Xyrillian starship captain in Enterprise's 'Unexpected,' hid Randy Oglesby under mottled alien skin and a bald cranium prosthetic. The role came early in a run of Enterprise guest appearances that culminated in his multi-episode turn as the Xindi conspirator Degra.

knownFor: Widely credited as having played more distinct named Star Trek characters than any other actor, appearing across every live-action series from The Next Generation through Enterprise.
reveal: Under Gul Danar's ridged Cardassian neck and cranial plating is Vaughn Armstrong, in one of his earliest franchise appearances, cast in only the second episode of Deep Space Nine. He went on to return again and again under fresh prosthetics as Klingons, Romulans, and more across four different series.

knownFor: Widely credited as having played more distinct named Star Trek characters than any other actor, appearing across every live-action series from The Next Generation through Enterprise.
reveal: Seskal, the defected Cardassian rebel who ferries Garak, Damar, and Kira toward a doomed meeting on Cardassia Prime, is Vaughn Armstrong under another round of Cardassian prosthetics. It's one of several different Cardassians he wore across his many Deep Space Nine appearances, on top of his recurring human role as Admiral Forrest on Enterprise.

knownFor: Widely credited as having played more distinct named Star Trek characters than any other actor, appearing across every live-action series from The Next Generation through Enterprise.
reveal: The Klingon scientist Korath, who holds the time-travel technology Captain Janeway needs in Voyager's series finale 'Endgame,' is Vaughn Armstrong under Klingon forehead ridges. It came near the end of a decade-plus run of alien transformations across the franchise.

knownFor: Widely credited as having played more distinct named Star Trek characters than any other actor, appearing across every live-action series from The Next Generation through Enterprise.
reveal: Commander Korris, the escaped Klingon prisoner Worf confronts in TNG's 'Heart of Glory,' is Vaughn Armstrong in his very first Star Trek role — also his only appearance on The Next Generation. It made him the first male Klingon guest character on the series besides Worf himself.

knownFor: Widely credited as having played more distinct named Star Trek characters than any other actor, appearing across every live-action series from The Next Generation through Enterprise.
reveal: Dr. Telek R'Mor, the Romulan scientist who forms an unlikely bond with the stranded Voyager crew in 'Eye of the Needle,' is Vaughn Armstrong under Romulan ears and brow ridges. It is often singled out as one of his most affecting Star Trek guest turns.

knownFor: Widely credited as having played more distinct named Star Trek characters than any other actor, appearing across every live-action series from The Next Generation through Enterprise.
reveal: Lansor, a former Borg drone still telepathically linked to two others after their escape from the collective in 'Survival Instinct,' is Vaughn Armstrong under Borg implant prosthetics. It's one of many alien and cyborg transformations across his long run through Star Trek's 1990s series.

knownFor: A former professional ballet dancer who moved into acting after an injury, going on to play several different synth units across two seasons of Star Trek: Picard.
reveal: Synth unit 335, one of the artificial workers seen in Star Trek: Picard's second season, is played by Alex Diehl under the synths' pale, faintly artificial makeup. His ballet training shaped the controlled, deliberate physicality he gave the show's various synthetic characters.

knownFor: A former professional ballet dancer who moved into acting after an injury, going on to play several different synth units across two seasons of Star Trek: Picard.
reveal: F5 is one of the synth units destroyed in the Mars uprising that opens Star Trek: Picard's early episode 'Maps and Legends,' played by Alex Diehl under the synths' pale, faintly artificial makeup. He was cast as multiple different synth units within that same episode.

knownFor: A former professional ballet dancer who moved into acting after an injury, going on to play several different synth units across two seasons of Star Trek: Picard.
reveal: F7 is another of the synth units destroyed in the Mars uprising that opens Star Trek: Picard's early episode 'Maps and Legends,' played by Alex Diehl under the synths' pale, faintly artificial makeup. He took on multiple different synth designations within that same episode.

knownFor: A former professional ballet dancer who moved into acting after an injury, going on to play several different synth units across two seasons of Star Trek: Picard.
reveal: F8, the synth whose self-destruction drops the Mars planetary defense shield in the cold open of Star Trek: Picard's 'Maps and Legends,' is played by Alex Diehl. His background as a dancer informed the controlled physicality he brought to the role.

knownFor: A former professional ballet dancer who moved into acting after an injury, going on to play several different synth units across two seasons of Star Trek: Picard.
reveal: Harvey, a synth unit appearing in Star Trek: Picard's second season, is played by Alex Diehl, continuing a role he had already taken on multiple times in the show's first season under different synth designations.

knownFor: Renowned Royal Shakespeare Company stage actor globally known for playing Jean-Luc Picard across Star Trek: The Next Generation and its films, and Professor Charles Xavier in the X-Men film franchise.
reveal: Beneath Locutus of Borg's exposed skull plate, cybernetic eyepiece, and grey Borg pallor in 'The Best of Both Worlds' is Patrick Stewart, kept just recognizable enough by Michael Westmore's design to land the episode's horror of Picard's own face trapped inside the assimilation. Stewart briefly reprised the look years later in the film Star Trek: First Contact.

knownFor: Better known as Captain Jean-Luc Picard, his own signature Star Trek role, and as Professor Charles Xavier in the X-Men film franchise.
reveal: To talk down the D'Arsay archive's sun-goddess persona, Picard dons a full ceremonial mask to become Korgano, the mythic moon-god hunter, his face entirely covered for the episode's climax. It's a rare TNG moment where Stewart, an actor built around facial expression, plays an entire dramatic confrontation from behind a mask.

knownFor: Better known as Captain Jean-Luc Picard, his own signature Star Trek role, and as Professor Charles Xavier in the X-Men film franchise.
reveal: In the cold open of 'The Defector,' Data studies Shakespeare's Henry V on the holodeck, and Stewart plays the battle-worn common soldier Michael Williams under heavy makeup rather than his usual clean-shaven captain, a part he requested for his love of the play. The weathered, aged face on screen briefly hides the Enterprise's captain in plain sight.

knownFor: Better known as Captain Jean-Luc Picard, his own signature Star Trek role, and as Professor Charles Xavier in the X-Men film franchise.
reveal: As Kamin, Picard lives an entire second life on a dying planet, aged by the makeup department through forty simulated years with a makeup call that ran six hours, the longest of any actor across the show's run. By the episode's end the frail, dying old man on screen bears little resemblance to the captain who began it.

reveal: Under a Bajoran nose-ridge appliance, Sloyan plays the Bajoran exobiologist who studied, and mistreated, a young shapeshifting Odo decades earlier. It's the lightest disguise of the four distinct Star Trek characters Sloyan played across three different series.

knownFor: Known as one of Star Trek's most frequently recurring guest actors, having played four separate named characters across The Next Generation, Deep Space Nine and Voyager.
reveal: Sloyan spends most of 'Data's Day' disguised as a Vulcan ambassador before Romulan ridges are revealed beneath the deception: Setal, a Romulan operative posing as a defector. It's built to fool the Enterprise crew as much as the audience, one of four distinct Star Trek faces Sloyan wore.

knownFor: Alan Scarfe was a British-born, Canadian-trained classical actor with a long career at Ontario's Stratford Festival alongside decades of film and TV guest roles.
reveal: Under Tokath's Romulan brow ridge and combed-forward hairpiece is Alan Scarfe, who played the quietly ruthless commander of a hidden internment camp holding aged Klingon prisoners in 'Birthright, Part II.' The alteration is confined to the forehead appliance and hair -- his own face and stage-trained voice carry the rest of the performance.

knownFor: Philip Anglim originated the title role in the 1979 Broadway production of 'The Elephant Man,' earning a Tony Award nomination.
reveal: This is the same Philip Anglim playing Bareil's ruthless mirror-universe counterpart, the Bajoran nose prosthetic unchanged but the performance turned cold and calculating. The actor who once appeared unaided as Joseph Merrick spent this guest turn behind a single reused facial appliance.

reveal: Under V'Lar's pointed ears and upswept Vulcan brow is Dublin-born actress Fionnula Flanagan, whose stage and screen career stretches back to the 1960s. The elegant charm the script called for came from an actress decades into a career of playing exactly that kind of role, ears and brow the only things standing between her and the camera.

reveal: Cromwell later said the Karemma minister's prosthetics were so heavy and dominating that he stopped trying to imagine how an alien might feel and "just played him as a Human character and tried to handle the makeup." It is the same actor who, unmasked, played farmer Arthur Hoggett in Babe the same decade.

knownFor: American actor best known for playing farmer Arthur Hoggett in Babe (1995), which earned him an Academy Award nomination for Best Supporting Actor.
reveal: Westmore himself told Cromwell that the Yridian information dealer Shrek was a favorite build for the makeup crew, a gaunt, sunken-featured design a world away from the actor's own face. Cromwell would return to the franchise unmasked a few years later, playing Zefram Cochrane in First Contact.

knownFor: American stuntwoman and actress best known for playing telepath Lyta Alexander on Babylon 5 and starring as Barbara in the 1990 remake of Night of the Living Dead.
reveal: In her memoir Pleasure Thresholds, Tallman recalled the ordeal of Kiros's heavy glued-on facial prosthetic, joking that by the time makeup was done her hair "looked like Bozo, the Clown." Underneath it is the same stunt performer who spent the decade doubling Star Trek's leading women before stepping in front of the camera as the Arkarian mercenary herself.

knownFor: American stuntwoman and actress best known for playing telepath Lyta Alexander on Babylon 5 and starring as Barbara in the 1990 remake of Night of the Living Dead.
reveal: Tagana, an uncredited DS9 nurse, wears the standard Bajoran nose-ridge appliance that marks the species throughout the franchise. Off camera Tallman was the show's utility stunt double, filling in for Nana Visitor, Terry Farrell, and other DS9 leads across nearly a decade on the series.

knownFor: Jeffrey Combs is best known outside Star Trek for playing Herbert West in Stuart Gordon's Re-Animator horror film series.
reveal: Under the antennae, white hair, and blue skin tone is a stage-trained genre actor who became one of the franchise's most recognizable recurring aliens without ever appearing as himself on screen. Combs played Shran's clipped, short-tempered cadence through the same facial and cranial-antennae prosthetic across all four seasons of Enterprise.

knownFor: Jeffrey Combs is best known outside Star Trek for playing Herbert West in Stuart Gordon's Re-Animator horror film series.

He originated the role in 'To the Death' (1996) and this clone was killed off within the same story arc, replaced on screen by Weyoun 5.

knownFor: Jeffrey Combs is best known outside Star Trek for playing Herbert West in Stuart Gordon's Re-Animator horror film series.
reveal: This is the clone who replaces the original Weyoun after his death in 'Ties of Blood and Water' (1997), the episode that reveals the Vorta simply grow a new toady whenever one dies. Combs plays every numbered Weyoun under the same full facial prosthetic and pale contact lenses, distinguishing each clone only through voice and manner.

knownFor: Jeffrey Combs is best known outside Star Trek for playing Herbert West in Stuart Gordon's Re-Animator horror film series.
reveal: Introduced in 'Treachery, Faith and the Great River' (1998), this Weyoun is written as a 'defective' clone grown doubtful of the Dominion's war — a rare moment of interiority Combs plays entirely through vocal shading, since the shared facial prosthetic gives the clones no other way to look different from one another.

knownFor: Jeffrey Combs is best known outside Star Trek for playing Herbert West in Stuart Gordon's Re-Animator horror film series.
reveal: Weyoun 7 succeeds Weyoun 6 late in the final season and is killed by Worf; Combs performs the death scene under the same full Vorta facial appliance and contact lenses he wore for every numbered clone of the character.

knownFor: Jeffrey Combs is best known outside Star Trek for playing Herbert West in Stuart Gordon's Re-Animator horror film series.
reveal: The last of the numbered clones to appear on screen, Weyoun 8 carries the Dominion war effort through the DS9 series finale, 'What You Leave Behind.' Combs plays out Weyoun's final scenes fully unrecognizable under the same facial prosthetic he had worn as the character since 1996.

knownFor: Jeffrey Combs is best known outside Star Trek for playing Herbert West in Stuart Gordon's Re-Animator horror film series.
reveal: For this one-off mirror-universe version of his recurring DS9 character Brunt, Combs returns to the full Ferengi cranium-and-ear prosthetic in 'The Emperor's New Cloak.' It is one of at least ten different Star Trek roles the actor has played entirely in prosthetic makeup.

knownFor: Jeffrey Combs is best known outside Star Trek for playing Herbert West in Stuart Gordon's Re-Animator horror film series.
reveal: Tiron, a wealthy alien business associate of Quark's in the episode 'Meridian,' was Combs' first-ever Star Trek role, worn under a facial prosthetic he later said was physically uncomfortable to perform in. It set the pattern for a career built almost entirely under Star Trek makeup rather than his own face.

knownFor: Jeffrey Combs is best known outside Star Trek for playing Herbert West in Stuart Gordon's Re-Animator horror film series.
reveal: Penk, the bloodsport promoter who kidnaps Seven of Nine in 'Tsunkatse,' was Combs' only appearance on Voyager, played under a forehead prosthetic rather than his own face. It is one of at least ten different alien roles Combs has played across the Star Trek franchise, almost always in makeup.

knownFor: Veteran Hollywood stuntman credited as the only performer to have played Jason Voorhees, Leatherface, and Michael Myers on screen.

knownFor: Veteran Hollywood stuntman credited as the only performer to have played Jason Voorhees, Leatherface, and Michael Myers on screen.
reveal: As Kazon Maje Minnis in Voyager's "Alliances," Tom Morga disappeared under the weathered, ridged Kazon prosthetic makeup for one of dozens of small guest and stunt roles he played across the franchise. He also worked for years as an assistant stunt coordinator on Voyager, largely uncredited on screen.

knownFor: Veteran Hollywood stuntman credited as the only performer to have played Jason Voorhees, Leatherface, and Michael Myers on screen.
reveal: Soto's bald, bony-ridged Lethean skull hid Tom Morga under a headpiece Michael Westmore had reworked from another alien design on short notice. It was one of many Star Trek guest roles Morga took on across the 1990s, almost always unrecognizable under someone else's makeup.

knownFor: Veteran Hollywood stuntman credited as the only performer to have played Jason Voorhees, Leatherface, and Michael Myers on screen.
reveal: Madrat's scaly, multi-nostriled Markalian face, first designed for DS9's pilot years earlier, covered stunt performer Tom Morga in "Time's Orphan." He spent nearly three decades moving through Star Trek's alien roles largely unrecognized, on top of a long career doubling actors in other films.

knownFor: A Tony Award-winning stage actor, Auberjonois was best known on screen for playing the shapeshifting security chief Odo across all seven seasons of Deep Space Nine.
reveal: For the mirror-universe episode "Crossover," Auberjonois wore the same extensive facial prosthetic that smoothed his features into Odo's waxy, unfinished-looking shapeshifter texture every week on the main series — just playing a darker, more sinister version of the same disguised face. He later said he liked the character's mirror-universe wardrobe so much he asked for a version of it on the regular show.

knownFor: A Tony Award-winning stage actor, Auberjonois was best known on screen for playing the shapeshifting security chief Odo across all seven seasons of Deep Space Nine.
reveal: As the aging, holograph-hiding engineer Ezral in Enterprise's "Oasis," Auberjonois wore a different, comparatively basic Kantare prosthetic instead of his usual heavier Odo appliance. It was a rare non-Odo Star Trek guest turn, arriving about three years after Deep Space Nine had ended its run.

knownFor: A Tony Award-winning stage actor, Auberjonois was best known on screen for playing the shapeshifting security chief Odo across all seven seasons of Deep Space Nine.
reveal: In "Apocalypse Rising," Odo shapeshifts into a Klingon alias named Kodrak to infiltrate the Klingon Empire, giving Auberjonois the standard forehead-ridge Klingon prosthetic in place of his usual Odo makeup. Worf spends much of the episode ribbing Kodrak for being an unconvincingly mild-mannered warrior — a joke that plays on Auberjonois's normally reserved Odo persona showing through the new face.

knownFor: A Tony Award-winning stage actor, Auberjonois was best known on screen for playing the shapeshifting security chief Odo across all seven seasons of Deep Space Nine.
reveal: During Jadzia's zhian'tara rite of closure in "Facets," Odo's shapeshifting face was given a one-scene appliance — spots included — that remolded it into the likeness of former Dax host Curzon, letting Auberjonois play the role without leaving his own character's body. It's an unusual case of a prosthetic layered onto a prosthetic for a single sequence.

knownFor: Russ is best known for playing the Vulcan security officer Lieutenant Tuvok across all seven seasons of Star Trek: Voyager.
reveal: In Picard's series finale, Russ returned to play a Changeling infiltrator wearing Tuvok's face and pointed Vulcan ears, decades after he first originated the character. It reused the same style of Vulcan ear prosthetic he wore for most of a decade, now in service of a story about the character not being who he appeared to be.

knownFor: Russ is best known for playing the Vulcan security officer Lieutenant Tuvok across all seven seasons of Star Trek: Voyager.
reveal: Two years before he was cast as Tuvok, Russ appeared under a Klingon forehead prosthetic as the mercenary T'Kar in "Invasive Procedures," hired muscle helping an unjoined Trill try to steal the Dax symbiont. It's a small, early credit in a career that would soon make him a fixture of Star Trek's makeup chair.

reveal: Tony Todd built a recurring role as Worf's brother Kurn entirely under Klingon forehead ridges and braided hair across several TNG and DS9 appearances. It's the same unmistakable baritone that anchors the Candyman films, buried here under latex most casual viewers never connect to him.

knownFor: Spencer Garrett is a veteran American character actor with hundreds of guest and supporting credits across television and film since the 1980s.
reveal: Under a Vulcan/Romulan-style pointed-ear prosthetic, Garrett played a young Starfleet medical technician whose concealed part-Romulan ancestry drives the plot of TNG's 'The Drumhead.' Outside the appliance, he has spent decades as a familiar if often uncredited face of American television.

knownFor: Karl Wiedergott is a background and supporting actor who portrayed numerous alien crew members and guest characters across Star Trek: Voyager's seven-season run.
reveal: As Ameron, Wiedergott wore Ilari facial prosthetics for a speaking role in the political intrigue of the episode 'Warlord.' It was one of many alien disguises he wore across Voyager, making him one of the series' most frequently recurring yet least-recognized performers.

knownFor: Karl Wiedergott is a background and supporting actor who portrayed numerous alien crew members and guest characters across Star Trek: Voyager's seven-season run.
reveal: As Larr, Wiedergott wore prosthetic makeup marking him as one of the Menk, an oppressed indigenous people on the Ilari homeworld featured in 'Warlord.' It was another of the many alien parts he filled across Voyager, almost always hidden beneath makeup.

knownFor: Leigh J. McCloskey is a veteran American television actor with a long career spanning both daytime and primetime series since the 1970s.
reveal: Under Ilari facial prosthetics, McCloskey played the ruthless consciousness of a dead warlord who briefly seizes control of Kes's body in Voyager's 'Warlord.' The rest of his long screen career runs largely without such disguise.

knownFor: Leigh J. McCloskey is a veteran American television actor with a long career spanning both daytime and primetime series since the 1970s.
reveal: As Joran Dax, McCloskey wore the spotted Trill temple-and-neck markings to play a murderous former host of the Dax symbiont in the episode 'Equilibrium.' It is a rare made-up role in a career otherwise spent in front of the camera as himself.

knownFor: Canadian actress and Gemini Award winner best known for playing Annie Edison on the CBC children's series The Edison Twins (1984-1986).
reveal: Under the Drayan hairline appliance and concealing robes worn as First Prelate Alcia, this is the same actress who'd already won a Gemini for The Edison Twins. She later played Lt. Eiger in Star Trek: First Contact with no prosthetic at all, so the two Trek credits sit at opposite ends of the disguise spectrum.

knownFor: Drama Desk Award-winning stage actor and playwright, known for the 2016 Pulitzer-finalist play Gloria and his own play Inspired By True Events (2024).
reveal: Spahn began his on-screen career as a teenager under full Borg drone prosthetics, playing the leader of a group of stranded, unmatured Borg children in the episode 'Collective.' He went on to build his career almost entirely on New York stages instead of the screen.

knownFor: American character actor best known for playing serial killer Ted Bundy in a 2002 television film; also a Fangoria Chainsaw Award recipient for genre work.
reveal: Under full Cardassian neck-scale prosthetics as the tailor Hogue in DS9's 'Profit and Loss,' this is the same actor who would later become known to mainstream audiences for playing Ted Bundy on television. He also spent a season as a Borg drone in TNG's 'Descent, Part II,' making disguised alien work a recurring part of his early career.

knownFor: American character actor best known for playing serial killer Ted Bundy in a 2002 television film, with guest spots on 24, Charmed, and Heroes.
reveal: A decade after his Cardassian role on DS9, Burke returned to Star Trek as T'Pol's spurned Vulcan suitor Koss, disguised this time by nothing more than upswept brows and pointed ears. It's the same actor who played Ted Bundy on television and racked up guest spots across a string of genre shows.

knownFor: Chicago stage actor and screen character actor best known for playing the masked bank robber 'Grumpy' in The Dark Knight (2008) and Ryan Kavanaugh on the political drama Boss.
reveal: Goldring's Cardassian scales as Legate Kell in DS9's 'Civil Defense' hid the same actor who'd later be recognized as one of the bank robbers in The Dark Knight's opening heist. He started out as a puppeteer with Chicago's Cole Marionettes before moving into stage and screen acting, and died in 2022 at age 76.

knownFor: Chicago stage actor and screen character actor best known for playing the masked bank robber 'Grumpy' in The Dark Knight (2008) and Ryan Kavanaugh on the political drama Boss.
reveal: As the Alpha Hirogen commander Karr in 'The Killing Game,' Goldring wore heavy full-head Hirogen ridge prosthetics — a world away from the human face he'd later show as a Chicago political fixer on Boss and a masked robber in The Dark Knight. He worked the Chicago stage for decades before his death in 2022.

knownFor: The original Moogie, a Ferengi matriarch who defies the ban on women wearing clothes or earning profit in “Family Business.”
reveal: Andrea Martin originated Ishka beneath a wrinkled Ferengi headpiece and facial appliances that aged her into Quark and Rom’s mother. Makeup supervisor Michael Westmore adapted a stock Ferengi head with a new wrinkled face, aged hands, and additional body prosthetics for the episode.
